items.front: 9-4-1: Three items to determine when aircraft plans to dump fuel
items.back: 1) Route it will fly. 2) Altitude it will fly. 3) Weather conditions during the operation. All three must be determined.
When information is received that an aircraft plans to dump fuel — the obligation arises as soon as you learn of the plan, not when dumping begins.

items.front: 9-4-2: When can a fuel-dumping aircraft be asked to fly a different route?
items.back: EXCEPT when dumping for emergency reasons. Applies in both VFR and IFR conditions. Emergency dumps may not be rerouted.

items.front: 9-4-2: Emergency vs. non-emergency fuel dump routing
items.back: Non-emergency: may request different route (VFR or IFR). Emergency: may NOT request different route. The emergency exception is absolute.
At least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern being flown. Applies only in IFR conditions.

items.front: 9-4-3: Key numbers for IFR fuel dump altitude
items.back: 2,000 feet (minimum clearance above obstacle). 5 miles (obstacle search radius from route/pattern). These are exact thresholds, not guidelines.

items.front: 9-4-4: Four IFR separation methods from fuel-dumping aircraft
items.back: 1) 1,000 ft above (or 4-5-1 if greater). 2) 2,000 ft below. 3) 5 miles radar. 4) 5 miles laterally. Any one method is sufficient.
5 miles for radar-identified VFR aircraft, applied in accordance with paragraph 5-6-1. No vertical-only options for VFR.
ABOVE the dump aircraft: 1,000 ft (or 4-5-1 if greater). BELOW the dump aircraft: 2,000 ft flat — no cross-reference to 4-5-1. The below standard is stricter.
1) Controller in contact: inform other controllers/facilities. 2) Concerned facilities: broadcast advisory every 3 minutes. 3) When dump ends: broadcast terminating advisory.
ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING IN PROGRESS OVER (location) AT (altitude) BY (type aircraft) (flight direction).
ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT. FUEL DUMPING OVER (location) TERMINATED.
